title: Self reward design with fine-grained interpretability

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - - SELF REWARD DESIGN WITH FINE GRAINED INTER PRETABILITY

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Transparency and fairness issues in Deep Reinforcement Learning may stem from the black-box nature of deep neural networks used to learn its policy, value functions etc. This paper proposes a way to circumvent the issues through the bottomup design of neural networks (NN) with detailed interpretability, where each neuron or layer has its own meaning and utility that corresponds to humanly understandable concept. With deliberate design, we show that lavaland problems can be solved using NN model with few parameters. Furthermore, we introduce the Self Reward Design (SRD), inspired by the Inverse Reward Design, so that our interpretable design can (1) solve the problem by pure design (although imperfectly) (2) be optimized via SRD (3) perform avoidance of unknown states by recognizing the inactivations of neurons aggregated as the activation in _wunknown_ .

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Reinforcement Learning (RL) and Deep Neural Network (DNN) have recently been integrated to solve problems with remarkable performance. The deep reinforcement learning greatly improves the state-of-the-art of control and, in the words of Sutton & Barto (2018), _learning from interaction_ . Among the well-known successes are (1) the Deep Q-Network (Mnih et al. (2015)) which enabled machine to play Atari Games with incredible performance, and (2) AlphaGo which is capable of playing notoriously complex game of Go (Silver et al. (2016)), which has also been further developed and popularized as being capable of defeating human at pro level. Although DNN has proven to possess great potentials, it is a blackbox that is difficult to interpret. To address this difficulty, various works have emerged, thus we have a host of different approaches to eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI); see surveys Arrieta et al. (2020); Gilpin et al. (2018); Tjoa & Guan (2020). They have shed some lights into the inner working of a DNN, but there may still be large gaps to fill. Note that there is no guarantee that interpretability is even attainable, especially when context-dependent interpretability can be subjective.

In this paper, we propose the Self Reward Design, a non-traditional RL solution that combines highly interpretable human-centric design and the power of DNN. Our robot (representing any artificial agent) rewards itself through purposeful design of DNN architecture, enabling it to solve problem **without training** . The solution might be sub-optimal, but the use of trainable DNN modules (we use pytorch, specifically) addresses the problem. We show that performance is improved while interpretability is retained.

This paper is arranged as the following. We start with clarifications. Then we briefly go through related works that inspire this paper. Then our interpretable design and SRD training idea are demonstrated with a 1D toy example, RobotFish. Following that, we introduce SRD on the main robot navigation problem on the 2D _lavaland_ with a large focus on interpretable design, followed by SRD training and unknown avoidance. Its experimental results will be discussed in the section after, and finally, we conclude with limitations and future works.

## 2 THIS PAPER FOCUSES HEAVILY ON INTERPRETABLE HUMAN DESIGN

**What exactly is this paper about?** Demonstrations of how two reinforcement learning problems are solved in an interpretable manner through self-reward mechanism. Readers will find how we

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

design different components taylored to different problem components. See appendix A.1 for more remarks: the paper has been heavily reorganized to direct readers’ focus on _interpretable design_ .

**But what is interpretability?** While there may be many ways to talk about interpretability, interpretability in the context of this paper is fine-grained, i.e. we go all the way down to _directly manipulating weights and biases_ of DNN modules. DNN modules are usually optimized using gradient descent from random initialization, thus the resulting weights are hard to interpret. In our SRD model, the meaning and purpose of each neural network component can be explicitly stated with respect to the environmental and model settings.

**How do we compare our interpretability** with existing explainable deep RL methods? Since we directly manipulate the weights and biases, our interpretability is at a very low level of abstraction, unlike post-hoc analysis e.g. saliency (Greydanus et al. (2018)) or semantically meaningful high level specification such as reward decomposition (Juozapaitis et al. (2019)). In other words, we aim to be as transparent as possible, allowing users to understand the model from the most basic unit.

**Baseline** . Our main result on 2D robot lavaland example achieves a high performance of approximately 90% accuracy with 10% randomness to allow for exploration. Hence, we believe comparing accuracy performance with other RL methods is irrelevant, and, most importantly, distracting from our focus of interpretability. If possible, _we want to compare the level of interpretability_ . Quantitative comparison is tricky, then how do we compare? What baseline to use? We have in fact answered this question in the previous paragraph: our interpretability is fine-grained as we directly manipulate weights and biases.

**Different design for different contexts** . There is probably no cookie-cutter framework for SRD. Solution design that is interpretable according to the above definition necessarily and heavily depends on the context, i.e. the shape of the system, thus we might need different formula of losses. This paper demonstrates how purposeful and deliberate designs work on two RL problems. In our robot fish example, the task is simply to survive, hence the model rewards actions that encourages survival e.g. “eat when hungry”. In 2D robot lavaland example, each tile is considered as a potential checkpoint to reach before reaching the final target, thus the model uses tile-based module that rewards the tile relative to other tiles: the preferred tile is scored higher.

## 2.1 RELATED WORKS: FROM RL TO DEEP RL TO SRD

**RL imperfect human design** . RL system can be set up by human manually specifying the rewards. Unfortunately, human design can easily be imperfect since the designers might not necessarily grasp the full extent of complex problem. For RL agents, designers’ manual specification of rewards are fallible, subject to errors and problems such as _negative side effect of a misspecified reward_ (Clark & Amodei (2016)) and _reward hacking_ (Russell & Norvig (2010)). Dylan’s _inverse reward design_ (IRD) paper (Hadfield-Menell et al. (2017)) addresses this problem directly: it allows a model to learn beyond what imperfect designers specify. An important component of our solution to imperfect designer problem is the implementation of _unknown avoidance_ , particularly _wunknown_ in our 2D robot. Also see appendix A.2 for Reward Design Problem (RDP).

**From RL to Deep RL** . Not only is human design fallible, it might be very difficult, especially for complex problems. In the introduction, we mention that Deep RL (DRL) solves this by combining RL and the power of DNN. However, DRL rewards have been produced by the black-box: they become difficult to understand and thus explainable DRL emerges to address that problem.

RL papers that address explainability/interptretability problems have been compiled in survey papers (eg. Heuillet et al. (2021); Puiutta & Veith (2020)). Saliency, a common XAI method, has been applied to visualize deep RL’s mechanism (Greydanus et al. (2018)). Relational deep RL uses a relational module that not only improves the agent’s performance on StarCraft II and Box-World, but also provides visualization on the attention heads useful for interpretability (Zambaldi et al. (2019)). Other methods to improve interpretability include reward decomposition, in which each part of the decomposable reward is semantically meaningful (Juozapaitis et al. (2019)); do refer to the survey papers for several other ingenious designs and investigations into the interpretability of deep RL.
**From DRL to SRD** . Our model is called _self reward_ design because our robot computes its own reward, similar to DRL computation of Q-values. However, human design is necessary to put con-

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

straints on how self-rewarding is performed so that interpretability is maintained. Human designer has the responsibility of understanding the problems, dividing the problems into smaller chunks and then finding the relevant modules to plug into the design in a fully interpretable way (see for example how we use convolution layer to create the _food location detector_ , section 2.1).

**Computational Efficiency of SRD** . The models only use modules from standard DNN modules such as convolution (conv), deconvolution (deconv) and fully-connected (FC) layers with few trainable parameters (e.g. only 180 parameters in Robot2NN). With proper choice of initial parameters, we can skip the long, arduous training and optimization processes that are usually required for deep learning to learn unseen concepts. We trade off the time spent on training algorithm with the time spent on human design, thus addressing what Kahn et al. (2018) called _sample inefficiency_ (the need for large dataset hence long training time) in a human-centric way.

## 2.2 OTHER RELEVANT CONCEPTS

**Self-supervised Learning** . DRL like Value Prediction Network (VPN, Oh et al. (2017)) is selfsupervised. Exploration-based RL algorithm is applied so that data are gathered real-time for training: the model thus optimizes its reward function. Our model is similar in this aspect. Unlike VPN, however, our interpretable design avoids all the abstraction of DNN. Our SRD training is also selfsupervised in the sense that we do not require datasets with ground-truth labels. Instead, we induce semantic bias via interpretable components to achieve the correct solutions. Also see appendix A.2.

**Imagination component** . Our pipeline includes several rollouts of possible future trajectories similar to existing RL papers that use imagination components, with differences as the following. Compared to Kalweit & Boedecker (2017) which optimizes towards target value ˆ _yi_ = _ri_ + _γQ[′] i_ +1[(heavily] abbreviated), SRD (1) is similar because agent updates on every imaginary sample available, but (2) has different, context-dependent loss computation. Also see appendix A.2 for more references.

## 3 ROBOT FISH: 1D TOY EXAMPLE

**Problem setting** . To broadly illustrate the idea, we use a one-dimensional model Fish1D with Fish Neural Network (FishNN) deliberately designed to survive the simple environment. Robot Fish1D has _energy_ which is represented by a neuron labelled _F_ . Energy diminishes over time. If the energy reaches 0, it dies. The environment is _env_ = [ _e_ 1 _, e_ 2 _, e_ 3] where _ei_ = 0 _._ 5 indicates there is a food at position _i_ and no food if _ei_ = 0. The fish is always located in the first block of _env_ , fig. 1(A). In this problem, ‘food here’ scenario is _env_ = [0 _._ 5 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] which means the food is near the fish. Similarly, ‘food there’ scenario is _env_ = [0 _,_ 0 _._ 5 _,_ 0] or _env_ = [0 _,_ 0 _,_ 0 _._ 5], which means the food is somewhere ahead and visible. ‘No food’ scenario is _env_ = [0 _,_ 0 _,_ 0].

**Fish1D’s Actions** . (1) ‘eat’: recover energy _F_ when there is food in its current position. (2) ‘move’: movement to the right. In our implementation, ‘move’ causes _env_ to be rolled left. If we treat the environment as an infinite roll tape and _env_ as fish vision’s on the 3 immediately visible blocks, then the food is available every 5 block.

Figure 1: Robot Fish setting. The fish is half full/hungry, _F_ = 0 _._ 5. Left: no food. Middle: food on _env_ 3, thus “food there” neuron lights up. Right: food on _env_ 1, thus “food here” neuron lights up.

**How to design an interpretable component of neural network?** First, we want the fish to be able to distinguish 3 scenarios previously defined: food here, food there and no food. Suppose we want a neuron that strongly activates when there is food nearby (name it _food here_ neuron, _fh_ ), another neuron that strongly activates when there is a food nearby (name it _food there_ neuron, _ft_ ), and we want to represent the no-food scenario as ‘neither _fh_ and _ft_ respond’. How do we design a layer with two neurons with the above properties? We use 1D convolution layer and _selective activation_ function as _σsa_ ( _x_ ) = _ϵ/_ ( _||x||_[2] + _ϵ_ ), as the following.

When there is food near the fish, we get _yfh_ = _convfh_ ( _env_ ) = [1 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] _∗_ [0 _._ 5 _,_ 0 _,_ 0] _−_ 0 _._ 5 = 0 where _∗_ denotes the convolution operator, so _afh_ = _σsa_ ( _yfh_ ) = 1. Together, they form the first layer called the Food Location Detector (FLD). Generally, we have used

**Interpretable FishNN** . To construct the neural network responsible for the fish’s actions (eat or move), we need one last step: connecting the neurons plus fish’s internal state (energy) (altogether [ _afh, aft, F_ ]) to the action output vector [ _eat, move_ ] _≡_ [ _e, m_ ] through FC layer, as shown in fig. 2 blue dotted box. The FC weights are chosen meaningfully e.g. ‘eat when hungry and there is food’ and to avoid scenarios like ‘eat when there is no food’. This is interpretable through manual weight and bias setting.

Figure 2: FishNN architecture, _δ >_ 0 a small value.

**Is FishNN’s decision correct?** The prefrontal cortex (PFC) decides whether FishNN’s decision is correct or not. PFC is seen in fig. 2 green dotted box. The name ‘PFC’ is only borrowed from the neuroscience to reflect our idea that this part of FishNN is associated with internal goals and decisions, similar to real brain (Miller et al. (2002)). How do we construct PFC? First, define _threshold activation_ as _τ_ ( _x_ ) = _Tanh_ ( _LeakyReLU_ ( _x_ )). Then PFC is constructed deliberately in the same way FishNN is constructed in an interpretable way as the following.

First, aggregate states from FishNN into a vector _v_ 0 = [ _afh, aft, F, e, m_ ]. This will be the input to PFC. Then, _v_ 0 is processed using _convP F C_ followed by softmax and threshold activation. The choice of weights and biases can be seen in table 2 in appendix B. With this design, we achieve meaningful activations _v_ 1 = [ _e_ 1 _, m_ 1 _, ex_ ] as before. For example, _e_ 1 is activated when “there is food and the fish eats it when it is hungry”, i.e. _fh_ = 1 _, F <_ 1 and _e_ is activated relative to _m_ 1. The output of PFC is binary vector [ _True, False_ ] = [ _T, F_ ] obtained from passing _v_ 1 through a FC layer _FCP F C_ . In this implementation, we have designed the model such that the activation of any _v_ 1 neuron is considered a _True_ response; otherwise it is considered false. This is how PFC judges whether FishNN’s action decision is correct.

**Self reward optimization** . As seen above, fish robot has FishNN that decides on an action to take and the PFC that determines whether the action is correct. Is this system already optimal? Yes, if we are only concerned with the fish’s survival, since the fish will not die from hunger. However, it is not optimal with respect to average energy. We optimize the system through standard DNN backpropagation with the following self reward loss

_loss_ = _CEL_ ( _z, argmax_ ( _z_ )) (2)

where CEL is the Cross Entropy Loss and _z_ = Σ _[mem] i_ =1[[] _[T, F]_[]] _[i]_[is][the][accumulated][decision][over] _mem_ = 8 iterations to consider past actions (pytorch notation is used). The ground-truth used in the loss is _argmax_ ( _z_ ), computed by the fish itself: hence, self-reward design.

**Results** . Human design ensures survival i.e. problem is solved correctly. Initially, fish robot decides to move rather than eat food when _F ≈_ 0 _._ 5, but after SRD training, it will prefer to eat whenever

## 4 2D ROBOT IN LAVALAND

**The lavaland problem** . Like Dylan’s IRD paper, we use lavaland as the test bed. Given a map with either dirt (brown), grass (green), lava (red) and target (yellow) tiles and the robot’s starting point (blue x), as shown in _xattn_ of fig 4, the robot chooses to move either UP, DOWN, LEFT or RIGHT to reach the target. We treat lava as the “unrecognized tile” that the designer forgets to take into account (imperfect designer). Problem is considered solved if the target is reached within 36 steps. Robot should prefer not to step on grass tiles but must be designed to avoid lava tile at all cost.

Figure 4: Robot2NN and PFC. Robot2NN makes 2D robot’s action decisions. _xattn_ (the whole visible map) and _wself_ (the agent’s position) are the direct input to the model. In _v_ 1 _, v_ Σ, red values are positive (desirable), white zero and blue negative (not desirable). 2D robot’s PFC (green dotted box) resembles RobotFish’s PFC in their function of accumulating decision values. [ _wgrass_ ]14 _,_ [ _wgrass_ ]23 are examples of strong activations that are interpretable through tile-based module.

**Interpretable design** . Like fish robot, we manually choose weights and bias for 2D robot’s neural network to solve the problem. Convolutional layers etc are used by Robot2NN to score the tiles (red better, blue worse), i.e. robot creates a _favourability gradient_ . Robot then chooses action that generally moves it from blue to red regions.

They induce the biases that designers input into the model in a simple, interpretable way. We also define the _unknown avoidance_ parameter _ua ≥_ 0.

**Making one plan** . Each plan is a series of action, and each action is chosen as the following. Set the specific values for the target tile and tile of the original position to _v_ 0 and _−v_ 0 respectively, where _v_ 0 = _max{|v_ Σ _|}_ is computed without backpropagation gradient to prevent any complications. Finally, to incorporate lava avoidance, or generally avoidance of anything previously unseen, _v_ Σ _← v_ Σ _∗_ (1 _− wunknown_ )+ _−ua ∗ v_ 0 _∗ wunknown_ . From the current position, robot’s neighbouring [ _v_ Σ] _i_ where _i_ = _up, down, left, right_ values are collected, and neighbours with the top two values are chosen. From the top two choices, randomly choose one of them with a 9 to 1 odds, favouring the action with higher value. The randomness is to encourage exploration. **Imagining multiple plans for SRD optimization** . Robot makes plans by imagining _nplans_ = 4 different trajectories to reach the target. It plans and executes the plan with the highest _vplan_ . Like

## 5 EXPERIMENT AND RESULTS

**Experimental setup and comparison** . We experiment on several initial settings as the following. For each experiment, we randomly generate and save 4096 maps to evaluate the performance of both standard design and SRD trained model, and similarly 4096 maps for SRD training. Note: All codes are included, including codes for creating animated gifs of robot traversing the lavaland. See appendix C.1 regarding multiple trials of the same experiments we conducted for reproducibility (project B, C, D etc).

**Reaching targets without training, optimized by training** . Table 1 shows that even without training (“No SRD” columns), our interpretable designs have enabled relatively high rate of problem solving. With SRD, the accuracies are further improved.

Table 1: Accuracy comparisons for Project A, project Compare A, project With-Lava-A and project Lava NOAV A. _Acc_ = _nreached target/_ 4096. “No SRD” indicates no training: reasonable accuracy is attainable purely with design. SRD training generally increases the accuracies. Starred values reflect failure modes, matching their respective histograms; see fig. 6 and 7.

**Project A: standard design and SRD on maps without lava tiles** . The fraction of grass tiles is 0 _._ 3. No robot will be spawned at the target immediately. Here, we use _pdirt,grass_ = 0 _._ 2 _, −_ 0 _._ 8 chosen empirically. For training, we only run through 1 epoch, taking a very short time to complete (less than 0.5 hour per project without GPU). Fig. 6(B) shows the cumulative histogram of the number of tiles traversed with _ptarget_ = 2.

**Compare A: model is more focused on the target** . This setting is similar to Project A, but we use a more extreme parameter setting, i.e. set _ptarget_ = 10. The local reward of the target tile is increased, and from table 1, we see that this generally increases the accuracies of both standard and SRD trained model (more likely to reach the target tile). The value seems to produce more unstable results as well, i.e. we empirically observe more failure modes.

**Project Lava A** . In this experiment, 0 _._ 1 of the tiles randomly assigned as lava and unknown avoidance _ua_ = 2. We also set _ptarget_ = 10 thus, similar to _Compare A_ we see higher accuracy but some failure modes as well. The model is designed to not recognize a lava tile, so a lava tile will activate the robot’s Robot2NN _wunknown_ at lavas’ positions. Fig. 7(A) is just an example of how robot successfully avoids lava, while fig. 7(C) lava cumulative histograms show that there are nearly zero lava tiles traversed.

**Project Lava NOAV A** . Project Lava NOAV A is similar to Project Lava A, but we set _ua_ = 0, i.e. no unknown avoidance. The results are clear, we see in 7(D) that robot will traverse the lava tiles without much regards.

## 6 CONCLUSION, LIMITATION AND FUTURE DIRECTIONS

_Generalizability and scalability_ . An important limitation to this design is the possible difficulty in creating specific design for very complex problems, for example, computer vision problems with high-dimensional state space. Problems with multitude of unknown variables might be difficult to factor into the system, or, if they are factored in, the designers’ imperfect understanding of the variables may create a poor model. Future works on applying interpretable SRD on complex systems might push Deep RL community into designing systems that aim not only to attain high performance, but also to be as highly interpretable as our design. **Controlling Weights and Biases** . A

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

future study on regularizing weights may be interesting. While we have shown that our design provides a good consistency between the interpretable weights before and after training, it is not surprising that the combination of small differences in weights can yield different final weights that still perform well. So far, it is not clear how different parameters affect the performance of a model, or if there are any ways to regularize the training of weight patterns towards something more interpretable and consistent. So far, there is no guarantee for convergence either, and it may be difficult to provide a general proof, since the design is heavily dependent on the context. A **technical limitation** is the lack of APIs that perform the exact operations we may need to parallelize the imaginative portions of SRD optimization.

**Conclusion** . Finally, we have demonstrated interpretable neural network design with fine-grained interpretability. Human experts purposefully design models that solve specific problems based on their knowledge of the problems. SRD is introduced as a way to improve performance on top of the designers’ imperfection. The system is highly interpretable with manual adjustment of weights and biases. It is also efficient since very few weights are needed compared to traditional DNN.

## ETHICS STATEMENTS

There is no specific ethical statement present. We demonstrate methods for designing neural networks for RL problem in a transparent way. We believe each part is transparently presented, and, if the paper is insufficient, the code in supp. materials should fully complements each description made in this paper.

## REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENTS

All codes are available in the supp. materials, to be released to public repository in case of acceptance. Results are tested on different random number seeds, and irregularities or mode collapses have been presented clearly in the paper as well (see for example the annotations made on the cumulative histograms).

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a novel and thought-provoking approach to interpretable reinforcement learning through Self Reward Design (SRD), emphasizing fine-grained interpretability by directly designing neural network weights and biases. It demonstrates a compelling vision of human-in-the-loop reward shaping where transparency is prioritized via architectural and parametric control. The work is creative in its use of toy domains and deliberate design choices, and it effectively illustrates how interpretability can be embedded from the ground up rather than retrofitted.

Overview issues:

- Interpretability claims lack quantitative or comparative evaluation: The paper asserts fine-grained interpretability as a core contribution but provides no quantitative metrics or systematic comparison to baseline methods that also claim interpretability (e.g., saliency maps, attention visualization, or reward decomposition). While the authors acknowledge the difficulty of quantifying interpretability, they dismiss comparison outright rather than proposing even a qualitative rubric or human study to evaluate the clarity of their designs. Without such evaluation, the claim that this method offers superior interpretability remains subjective and unvalidated. A minimal fix would be to introduce a structured interpretability assessment, such as a feature-attribution consistency test or side-by-side expert evaluation against post-hoc XAI methods.

- Circularity in self-reward mechanism undermines objectivity: The self-reward design uses the agent’s own judgment (via the PFC module) as ground truth in the loss function (e.g., equation 2 and 4), creating a potentially circular optimization loop. In the Fish1D example, the PFC is manually designed to recognize ‘correct’ actions, then used to train the policy to match those actions—effectively reinforcing the designer’s prior assumptions without external validation. This risks conflating consistency with correctness: the agent may become better at self-justifying its behavior rather than improving performance in any objective sense. The paper needs to clarify how the PFC’s criteria are validated independently or demonstrate that SRD training leads to measurable improvements beyond the initial design.

- Insufficient ablation of interpretability components: The paper does not isolate the contribution of fine-grained interpretability to learning performance. There is no ablation showing what happens when interpretable components (e.g., hand-designed convolutions or tile-based modules) are replaced with standard learned counterparts. Without such comparison, it is unclear whether the success stems from the interpretability itself or simply from the inductive biases introduced by the architecture. For instance, high performance in lavaland could be due to the favorable gradient design rather than the transparency of weights. The authors should include ablations that disable or randomize interpretable modules to assess their necessity and impact.

- Overreliance on toy domains limits generalizability claims: The experiments are confined to highly simplified environments (1D fish, 2D grid worlds) with hand-engineered features and known dynamics. While useful for illustration, these domains do not reflect the complexity, partial observability, or high dimensionality of real-world RL problems. The paper’s conclusion suggests broader applicability, but no evidence is provided that the approach scales beyond these controlled settings. The lack of testing on even slightly more complex benchmarks (e.g., MiniGrid or Procgen) weakens the contribution. To strengthen the claims, the authors should either qualify the scope or demonstrate extension to a moderately complex domain with learned features.

- Unknown avoidance mechanism is underspecified and untested: The unknown avoidance via $w_{unknown}$ is presented as a key safety feature inspired by Inverse Reward Design, but its behavior is only shown in one scenario (lava tiles). The threshold $\tau_{recog}$ and parameter $u_a$ are used without sensitivity analysis, and there is no evaluation of how robust the mechanism is to varying types of unknowns or distribution shifts. Moreover, the method assumes that unknowns produce low activation across all known tile detectors—an assumption that may fail in high-dimensional spaces where out-of-distribution inputs can activate known features spuriously. The paper should include failure mode analysis or stress tests to demonstrate the limits and reliability of this mechanism.

- Baseline comparisons are inadequate and selectively reported: The paper avoids comparing performance to standard RL methods, arguing that accuracy is secondary to interpretability. However, this ignores the need to demonstrate competitive efficacy even in toy domains. More critically, the comparison between 'No SRD' and 'SRD' models in Table 1 shows inconsistent improvements—some SRD-trained models perform worse (e.g., Compare A expt 2 and 4)—yet this is not discussed in depth. The variability suggests instability in the SRD optimization process, but the paper treats SRD as uniformly beneficial. A proper analysis should report statistical significance, variance across runs, and a discussion of failure cases to provide a balanced view of the method’s reliability.

- No ablation isolating interpretability from inductive bias: The paper attributes its success to fine-grained interpretability, but never disentangles this from the inductive biases introduced by the hand-designed architecture. For instance, the tile-based modules in Lavaland encode strong spatial and semantic priors—such as favoring paths toward the target—that could explain performance gains independently of interpretability. A critical test would be to replace the manually designed convolutional kernels (e.g., in DeconvSeq) with randomly initialized ones that preserve architecture but not human-readable structure, then compare performance and training dynamics. Without such an ablation, it is impossible to determine whether interpretability is driving results or merely accompanying effective design choices. The authors should add this comparison to clarify the actual source of their method's efficacy.

- Missing demonstration of interpretability in decision-making: The claim of fine-grained interpretability hinges on the ability to trace decisions back to specific weights and neurons, yet the paper does not show this in action. For example, in the Lavaland experiments, there is no analysis linking a specific policy change after SRD training—such as avoiding a particular tile—to adjustments in the corresponding $p_t$ or deconvolution weights. Readers are shown weight heatmaps before and after training, but not how those changes alter behavior. To substantiate interpretability claims, the authors should walk through a concrete case: pick one trajectory improvement post-SRD, identify which weights changed most, and explain how those changes reflect intelligible design logic rather than opaque optimization. This would transform a visual assertion into a demonstrated capability.

- No evaluation of self-reward objectivity or ground truth alignment: The self-reward mechanism uses internally generated labels (via PFC) as ground truth, but there is no check on whether these labels align with externally valid outcomes. In the Fish1D example, the PFC is designed to reward eating when hungry, but the paper does not verify that this actually improves survival or energy efficiency beyond the initial design. A minimal validation would be to compare SRD-trained policies against an oracle reward function derived from environmental success metrics (e.g., time to target, survival duration). Without such a comparison, the optimization may simply be amplifying designer assumptions rather than improving real performance. The authors should compute a correlation between SRD loss reduction and task success across episodes to demonstrate that self-reward tracks objective progress.

- Unknown avoidance lacks failure mode analysis: The $w_{unknown}$ mechanism is presented as a robust safety feature, but the paper only shows it working in the ideal case where lava tiles produce zero activation in all tile detectors. In practice, out-of-distribution inputs might partially activate known features (e.g., a new hazard resembling grass), causing $w_{unknown}$ to fail silently. The authors should test this by introducing a 'confounder tile'—say, a dark red tile similar to lava but not identical—and measure how often the agent misclassifies it as known. They could also sweep over $	au_{recog}$ values to show how sensitive detection is to threshold choice. This would reveal whether unknown avoidance is reliable or brittle under realistic uncertainty.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Introduce a structured evaluation of interpretability, either through human assessment, feature-attribution tests, or comparison to post-hoc XAI methods.

- Clarify and validate the self-reward mechanism to address circularity, potentially by introducing external correctness criteria or demonstrating behavioral improvement beyond initial design.

- Conduct ablation studies that isolate the impact of interpretable components on performance, replacing hand-designed modules with learned alternatives.

- Expand evaluation to at least one more complex or partially observed environment to test scalability and generalizability.

- Provide a thorough analysis of the unknown avoidance mechanism, including sensitivity to parameters and robustness to different types of out-of-distribution inputs.

comments.title: Self-reward loss uses invalid self-generated ground truth
comments.quote:
d loss

$loss=CEL(z,argmax(z))$ (2)

where CEL is the Cross Entropy Loss and $z=\Sigma_{i=1}^{mem}[T,F]_{i}$ is the accumulated decision over $mem=8$ iterations to consider past actions (pytorch notation is used). The ground-truth used in the loss is $argmax(z)$, computed by the fish itself: hence, self-reward
comments.explanation: The loss function CEL(z, argmax(z)) creates a tautological optimization objective: the model trains to match its own majority vote, reinforcing whichever decision was more frequent in the memory buffer regardless of correctness. This risks amplifying suboptimal behaviors rather than improving performance. For instance, if the initial policy favors moving over eating when hungry, the loss will reinforce that pattern. A valid self-reward mechanism must either use conditionally correct labels (e.g., 'eat when hungry and food present') or justify how self-majority voting leads to better outcomes. As written, the loss lacks an external anchor for correctness.

comments.title: Incorrect convolution output calculation in Fish1D example
comments.quote: $. When there is food near the fish, we get $y_{fh}=conv_{fh}(env)=[1,0,0]*[0.5,0,0]-0.5=0$ where $*$ denotes the convolution operator, so $a_{fh}=\sigma_{sa}(y_{fh})=1$. This
comments.explanation: The convolution calculation is ambiguous and potentially misleading. The expression [1,0,0]*[0.5,0,0] suggests element-wise multiplication, but convolution involves summation over element products. While the final result y_{fh}=0 is correct under full convolution (1×0.5 + 0×0 + 0×0 − 0.5 = 0), the notation fails to show the summation step, which may confuse readers about how convolutions work in neural networks. To avoid misinterpretation, the computation should explicitly include the dot product: y_{fh} = \sum_i w_i \cdot env_i - b_{fh}.

comments.title: Flawed PFC correctness criterion based on any activation
comments.quote:
$FC_{PFC}$. In this implementation, we have designed the model such that the activation of any $v_{1}$ neuron is considered a *True* response; otherwise it is considered false. This is how PFC judges whether FishNN’s action decision is correct.

Self r
comments.explanation: The PFC labels a decision as correct if *any* neuron in v_1 = [e_1, m_1, ex] activates, meaning even spurious or irrelevant activations (e.g., movement when eating is required) would be rewarded. This undermines the self-reward mechanism: the agent could receive positive feedback for incorrect actions as long as some component fires. The criterion should instead depend on specific, meaningful combinations—such as 'eat when hungry and food present'—to ensure alignment with survival goals. Without such logical constraints, the reward signal is unreliable and may optimize for noise.

comments.title: Misleading use of 'deconv' for standard convolution
comments.quote: Each deconv in DeconvSeq consists of 1 input and 1 output channel. It is fully interpretable through our manual selection of kernel weights: kernel has size 3 with center value 1 and side value 0.1 as seen in fig. 5(A).
comments.explanation: The term 'deconv' incorrectly implies transposed convolution or upsampling, but the described operation—a fixed 3×3 kernel applied with center weight 1 and side weights 0.1—is a standard convolutional filter. This misuses established deep learning terminology and may mislead readers into thinking the architecture performs upsampling. Since the method relies on precise interpretability, accurate naming is essential. 'DeconvSeq' should be renamed to 'ConvSeq' and all instances of 'deconv' replaced with 'convolutional layer' or 'filter' to reflect actual functionality.

comments.title: Ambiguous and incomplete tile notation overloading
comments.quote: ms. Tile  $t$  denotes the name of a tile, e.g. grass, but it also denotes its  $[0,1]$  normalized RGB value, e.g. for grass,  $t = [0,128,0] / 255$  or an array of tile values (they should
comments.explanation: The notation overloads t to mean both a tile type (a category) and its RGB vector, creating ambiguity in equations like x_{attn} - t (where t must be a vector) versus DS_t^5 (where t indexes modules). The sentence is also cut off mid-phrase, leaving the definition incomplete. This dual use introduces type confusion that undermines the paper's emphasis on fine-grained interpretability. To resolve this, distinct symbols should be used: e.g., t for tile type and c_t for its color vector. The quote should be revised to formally disambiguate these uses for clarity and correctness.

comments.title: Flawed unknown tile detection logic under partial activation
comments.quote: The unknown in our case is the lava tile that the imperfect human designer 'forgets' to account for.
comments.explanation: The claim that lava is 'forgotten' contradicts the mechanism used to detect it. The unknown avoidance signal w_{unknown} = [(1 - \Sigma_t w_t) > \tau_{recog}] assumes that known tile detectors (e.g., for grass, dirt, target) do not activate on lava. But lava has RGB [255,0,0]/255, and if the target tile is yellow ([255,255,0]/255), it may partially activate w_{target}, leading to \Sigma_t w_t > 0 and thus low w_{unknown}. Without evidence that tile detectors are designed to be orthogonal to red, the mechanism cannot reliably detect lava. The paper must either clarify that detectors are engineered to ignore lava-like colors or acknowledge that unknown detection fails under realistic partial activations.

comments.title: Inconsistent accuracy claim vs. Table 1 data
comments.quote: High performance  $90\%$  accuracy is attained, given  $10\%$  randomness is allowed. The
comments.explanation: The claim of '90% accuracy' does not reflect the majority of results in Table 1. Only two SRD-trained entries exceed 90% (0.941 and 0.909), while most are below—e.g., Project A peaks at 0.868 and With Lava A drops to 0.762*. Presenting 90% as typical overstates performance. Additionally, the phrase 'given 10% randomness' implies this accuracy is achieved despite exploration noise, but no analysis links the stochasticity to outcomes. The statement should be revised to report the actual range: 'Accuracies range from approximately 76% to 94%, with some SRD-trained models reaching near 90% under favorable settings.'

comments.title: Failure modes unexplained despite histogram references
comments.quote: Starred values reflect failure modes, matching their respective histograms; see fig. 6 and 7.
comments.explanation: The paper states that starred low-accuracy results (e.g., Compare A expt 2: 0.589, expt 4: 0.422) reflect failure modes visible in histograms, but Figures 6 and 7 do not contain data for these specific trials. There are no performance distributions, tile traversal stats, or trajectory analyses showing why these runs failed while others succeeded. This omission prevents readers from understanding SRD's instability under certain parameter settings. Either remove the reference to histograms or include new visualizations that directly depict the failure cases to support the claim.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about improved accuracy in lava experiments
comments.quote: . We also set  $p_{target} = 10$  thus, similar to Compare A we see higher accuracy but some failure modes as well. The mo
comments.explanation: The assertion that Project Lava A 'sees higher accuracy' is unsupported by Table 1. The best SRD result in With Lava A is 0.893, lower than Compare A's 0.941. Moreover, With Lava A expt 4 drops to 0.762*, indicating greater variance. The sentence conflates similar parameter settings (p_{target}=10) with similar outcomes, but the data show that lava presence introduces instability rather than consistent improvement. The claim should be revised to acknowledge that while p_{target} is increased similarly, performance is more variable and generally lower in lava conditions.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about model efficiency
comments.quote: It is also efficient since very few weights are needed compared to traditional DNN.
comments.explanation: The claim of efficiency due to 'very few weights' lacks quantitative support. The paper mentions 180 parameters in Robot2NN but provides no comparison to standard DNNs solving similar tasks. Without such benchmarks, the assertion of parameter efficiency remains unsubstantiated. Efficiency could also refer to computation, memory, or sample usage—none of which are evaluated. Given mixed performance in Table 1, the efficiency claim may not hold in practice. The statement should be tempered: 'It may be parameter-efficient due to structured connectivity, though direct comparisons to standard DNNs were not performed.'

comments.title: Conflation of consistency with convergence
comments.quote: So far, there is no guarantee for convergence either, and it may be difficult to provide a general proof, since the design is heavily dependent on the context.
comments.explanation: The lack of convergence is not just a theoretical challenge—it reflects a practical risk in the self-reward loop. Because the loss uses internally generated labels (via PFC), the training target is non-stationary and may drift or oscillate. The paper does not report training curves or loss stability, so readers cannot assess whether models converge in practice. This downplays a critical issue: if the self-reward signal doesn't stabilize, the policy may never settle. The statement should acknowledge empirical uncertainty: 'and empirical evidence of stable training dynamics was not provided.'

comments.title: Mischaracterization of DRL reward generation
comments.quote: However, DRL rewards have been produced by the black-box: they become difficult to understand and thus explainable DRL emerges to address that problem.
comments.explanation: This passage misrepresents standard DRL: the reward function is typically specified externally by the environment or designer, not generated by the neural network. What becomes opaque is the policy or value function, not the reward itself. By claiming DRL produces 'black-box rewards,' the paper misstates the motivation for SRD. While DRL can lead to uninterpretable decision-making, conflating learned policies with reward specification weakens the argument. The sentence should clarify that SRD targets interpretability of policy design, not reward engineering per se.

comments.title: Misleading comparison to self-supervised learning
comments.quote: DRL like Value Prediction Network (VPN, Oh et al. (2017)) is self-supervised. Exploration-based RL algorithm is applied so that data are gathered real-time for training: the model thus optimizes its reward function. Our model is similar in this aspect.
comments.explanation: Labeling VPN as 'self-supervised' because it gathers data via exploration misuses the term. Self-supervised learning refers to pretext tasks on unlabeled data (e.g., predicting missing inputs), not online experience collection in RL. VPN learns to predict future values from rewards—it does not optimize the reward function. The phrase 'optimizes its reward function' is incorrect and blurs a key distinction. The comparison should be reframed: 'Our model uses imagined rollouts similar to how VPN learns internal dynamics from experience,' to accurately reflect the analogy.

comments.title: Ambiguous plan score normalization
comments.quote: Thus we have obtained a plan and its score  $v_{plan} = \frac{1}{N_t}\Sigma_{i,j\in \xi}[v_{\Sigma}]_{i,j}$ , the mean of all values assigned to the chosen tiles where  $\xi$  is the trajectory (see fig. 4, orange arrows in  $v_{\Sigma}$ ).
comments.explanation: The denominator N_t is undefined. Given the context, it should represent the number of tiles in trajectory \xi, but this is not stated. Without clarification, the expression risks misinterpretation and hinders reproducibility. The normalization is essential for consistent loss computation across trajectories of different lengths. The quote should explicitly define N_t as |\xi|: 'v_{plan} = \frac{1}{|\xi|} \sum_{(i,j) \in \xi} [v_{\Sigma}]_{i,j}, the average value along the trajectory \xi.'

comments.title: Overstatement of design sufficiency in abstract
comments.quote: With deliberate design, we show that lavaland problems can be solved using NN model with few parameters.
comments.explanation: The word 'solved' overstates the capability of design alone. Table 1 shows that 'No SRD' models achieve only 76–87% accuracy, indicating suboptimal performance without training. Presenting this as 'solving' the problem misleads readers about the efficacy of pure design. The abstract should reflect the staged nature of the approach: 'With deliberate design, we construct NN models with few parameters that partially solve lavaland problems, with performance improved via SRD.'

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes Self Reward Design (SRD), a novel approach combining human-interpretable neural network design with self-supervised optimization for reinforcement learning. The method is demonstrated on toy examples (Robot Fish and 2D lavaland navigation), achieving good performance while maintaining fine-grained interpretability through deliberate weight/bias manipulation. While the concept is interesting and the implementation is transparent, the paper suffers from significant limitations in experimental validation, generalizability claims, and interpretability assessment.

The paper presents a creative approach to interpretable RL by designing neural networks where each component has semantic meaning. The core idea of combining human design with self-reward optimization is novel and the implementation details are thoroughly documented. The lavaland experiments demonstrate that interpretable designs can achieve reasonable performance without extensive training. However, the paper's claims are undermined by limited experimental scope, lack of quantitative interpretability metrics, and insufficient comparison with relevant baselines.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient experimental validation and generalizability: The paper only validates SRD on two simplistic toy problems: a 1D fish survival task and a 2D lavaland navigation task. These environments have extremely low-dimensional state spaces and manually engineered features. The authors acknowledge scalability concerns in the conclusion but provide no evidence that the approach would work on more complex RL benchmarks like Atari games, MuJoCo control tasks, or real-world robotic problems. Without such validation, claims about the broader applicability of SRD remain unsubstantiated. The paper should include experiments on at least one standard RL benchmark to demonstrate that the approach scales beyond toy examples.

- Lack of quantitative interpretability evaluation: The paper claims 'fine-grained interpretability' but provides no quantitative metrics to assess this property. While the authors show that weights can be manually interpreted, there is no systematic evaluation of interpretability. How do we measure whether interpretability is preserved after training? The paper mentions that 'center value remains the most prominent' but this is anecdotal. A proper evaluation should include metrics like neuron activation consistency, weight stability analysis, or human subject studies to verify that the semantic meanings remain clear and useful after optimization. Without such metrics, the interpretability claims remain qualitative and subjective.

- Inadequate baseline comparisons: The paper explicitly states that comparing accuracy with other RL methods is 'irrelevant and distracting' because they focus on interpretability. This is a problematic stance for a research paper. While interpretability is the main contribution, performance still matters. More importantly, the paper fails to compare against relevant XAI methods for RL like reward decomposition (Juozapaitis et al. 2019) or attention-based interpretability (Zambaldi et al. 2019). Without these comparisons, readers cannot assess whether SRD provides a better trade-off between performance and interpretability than existing approaches. The paper should include comparisons with at least one post-hoc interpretability method and one inherently interpretable RL method.

- Unclear self-reward mechanism formulation: The self-reward mechanism lacks a clear formal definition. Equation 2 uses cross-entropy loss with self-generated labels (argmax(z)), but it's unclear how this constitutes a 'reward' in the RL sense. The loss formulation appears more like a self-supervised learning objective than a reward signal. Similarly, Equation 4 for the 2D robot uses an arbitrary normalization and Tanh function without justification. The paper needs to clarify how these losses relate to standard RL reward functions and why this particular formulation was chosen. A more rigorous connection to RL theory would strengthen the contribution.

- Limited analysis of unknown avoidance mechanism: The unknown avoidance feature ($w_{unknown}$) is presented as a key contribution inspired by Inverse Reward Design, but its evaluation is superficial. The paper only shows that the robot avoids lava tiles when $u_a > 0$ (Figure 7), but doesn't analyze false positives/negatives or sensitivity to the threshold parameter $	au_{recog}$. How robust is this mechanism to different types of unknown states? What happens when unknown states resemble known ones? The paper should include a more thorough analysis of the unknown avoidance component, including failure cases and parameter sensitivity studies.

- Missing demonstration on standard RL benchmark: The paper validates SRD only on two toy environments (1D fish and 2D lavaland) with extremely low-dimensional state spaces. To substantiate claims about broader applicability, the paper needs at least one demonstration on a standard RL benchmark like CartPole, MountainCar, or a simple Atari game. This would show whether the interpretable design approach can scale beyond manually engineered problems. Without such validation, the contribution remains confined to toy examples.

- No quantitative interpretability metrics: The paper claims 'fine-grained interpretability' but provides only qualitative evidence. A complete evaluation should include quantitative metrics such as neuron activation consistency before/after training, weight stability measures, or human subject studies verifying semantic meanings remain clear. The current reliance on visual inspection of weights (Figure 5) and anecdotal observations is insufficient for a scientific claim about interpretability.

- Missing comparison with XAI baselines: The paper dismisses performance comparisons as 'irrelevant' but fails to compare against relevant interpretable RL methods like reward decomposition (Juozapaitis et al. 2019) or attention-based interpretability (Zambaldi et al. 2019). Without these comparisons, readers cannot assess whether SRD provides a better trade-off between performance and interpretability. The paper should include at least one post-hoc XAI method and one inherently interpretable RL method as baselines.

Recommendation: Major revision. The paper presents an interesting idea for interpretable RL but suffers from insufficient experimental validation, lack of quantitative metrics, and inadequate baseline comparisons. The core concept has merit but requires substantial additional work to demonstrate its value to the RL community.

Revision targets:

- Add experiments on at least one standard RL benchmark (e.g., a simple Atari game or MuJoCo task) to demonstrate scalability beyond toy problems

- Develop and report quantitative metrics for interpretability, such as neuron activation consistency before/after training or human evaluation of semantic clarity

- Include comparisons with relevant XAI baselines for RL, particularly reward decomposition and attention-based interpretability methods

- Provide a more rigorous formulation of the self-reward mechanism and its connection to standard RL theory

- Conduct a thorough analysis of the unknown avoidance mechanism including sensitivity analysis and failure mode characterization

comments.title: Contradictory claim about training requirement
comments.quote: Our robot (representing any artificial agent) rewards itself through purposeful design of DNN architecture, enabling it to solve problem without training. The solution might be sub-optimal, but the use of trainable DNN modules (we use pytorch, specifically) addresses the problem.
comments.explanation: The introduction contains a direct contradiction. It first claims the robot can solve problems "without training" through architectural design, but then immediately states that "trainable DNN modules" are used to "address the problem." This is logically inconsistent - if training is not needed, why are trainable modules required? The paper should clarify whether the initial solution works without training and training is only for performance improvement, or if training is essential from the start. Rewrite as: "Our robot rewards itself through purposeful DNN architecture design, providing an initial interpretable solution. While this baseline can solve problems without training, we further improve performance using trainable DNN modules while retaining interpretability."

comments.title: Circular reasoning in interpretability comparison
comments.quote:
ith respect to the environmental and model settings.

How do we compare our interpretability with existing explainable deep RL methods? Since we directly manipulate the weights and biases, our interpretability is at a very low level of abstraction, unlike post-hoc analysis e.g. saliency (*Greydanus et al. (2018)*) or semantically meaningful high level specification such as reward decomposition (*Juozapaitis et al. (2019)*). In other words, we aim to be as transparent as possible, allowing users to understand the model from the most basic unit.

Baseline. Our main result on 2D robot lavaland example achieves a high performance of approximately 90% accuracy with 10% randomness to allow for exploration. Hence, we believe comparing accuracy performance with other RL methods is irrelevant, and, most importantly, distracting from our focus of interpretability. If possible, *we want to compare the level of interpretability*. Quantitative comparison is tricky, then how do we compare? What baseline to use? We have in fact answered this question in the previous paragraph: our interpretability is fine-grained as we directly manipulate weights and biases.

Dif
comments.explanation: The authors use circular reasoning to justify not comparing against baselines. They first claim their method is more interpretable because it manipulates weights directly, then when asked how to compare interpretability levels, they answer that their method is more interpretable because it manipulates weights directly. This doesn't address the fundamental question of how to measure or compare interpretability across different approaches. The paper needs to propose concrete evaluation criteria for interpretability rather than restating its defining characteristic as the comparison metric. Rewrite the comparison section to propose specific interpretability metrics (e.g., human evaluation protocols, weight interpretability scores, or task understanding tests) that could be applied to both SRD and baseline methods.

comments.title: Self-reward loss formulation unclear
comments.quote:
$loss=CEL(z,argmax(z))$ (2)

where CEL is the Cross Entropy Loss and $z=\Sigma_{i=1}^{mem}[T,F]_{i}$ is the accumulated decision over $mem=8$ iterations to consider past actions (pytorch notation is used). The ground-truth used in the loss is $argmax(z)$, computed by the fish itself: hence, self-reward design.
comments.explanation: The self-reward loss formulation is problematic. Using $argmax(z)$ as both prediction and target in cross-entropy loss would result in zero loss, making optimization meaningless. The notation $\Sigma_{i=1}^{mem}[T,F]_{i}$ is also unclear - is this summing binary decisions? If the goal is to encourage consistent behavior, the loss should compare current decisions with past decisions or some internal goal, not with the argmax of the same accumulated vector. Clarify what $z$ represents and how the loss actually drives learning.

comments.title: Inconsistent unknown avoidance formula
comments.quote: the Boolean array  $w_{unknown} = [(1 - \Sigma_t w_t) > \tau_{recog}]$  to be treated as floating point numbers. From the formula, it can be seen that  $w_{unknown}$  aggregates the negation of known activations.
comments.explanation: The formula for $w_{unknown}$ has a logical inconsistency. It's described as a Boolean array using the '>' comparison operator, which would produce True/False values, but then states it should be "treated as floating point numbers." More critically, the expression $(1 - \Sigma_t w_t) > \tau_{recog}$ doesn't correctly implement the intended logic of detecting unknown tiles. If $w_t$ values are in [0,1] as suggested by the ABA definition, then $\Sigma_t w_t$ could exceed 1, making $(1 - \Sigma_t w_t)$ negative. The threshold $\tau_{recog} = 10^{-4}$ is very small, so this condition would rarely be true. The formula should be $w_{unknown} = [\Sigma_t w_t < \tau_{recog}]$ to properly detect tiles where no known pattern activates strongly.

comments.title: Undefined normalization function N
comments.quote:
$$
l o s s = \Sigma_ {i = 1} ^ {n _ {p l a n s}} (1 - T a n h (N [ v _ {p l a n, i} ])) ^ {2} \tag {4}
$$

where  $N[.]$  normalizes  $v_{plan}$  to the magnitude of 1, where normalization factor is computed without gradient to prevent complication.
comments.explanation: Equation 4 introduces a normalization function $N[.]$ that is never formally defined. The text states it "normalizes $v_{plan}$ to the magnitude of 1" but doesn't specify whether this is L2 normalization ($N[x] = x/\|x\|_2$), L-infinity normalization ($N[x] = x/\max|x|$), or some other scheme. This ambiguity is critical because the loss function's behavior depends entirely on this normalization. Given that $v_{plan}$ is defined as a mean value (not a vector), "normalization to magnitude 1" is particularly unclear - does this mean scaling to [0,1] range or something else? The normalization method needs to be explicitly specified.

comments.title: Inaccurate claim about DRL reward generation
comments.quote: However, DRL rewards have been produced by the black-box: they become difficult to understand and thus explainable DRL emerges to address that problem.
comments.explanation: This statement contains a fundamental misconception. In DRL, rewards are typically NOT produced by the black-box neural network - they are usually externally specified by the environment or task designer. The black-box refers to the policy/value function approximation, not reward generation. DRL inherits the same reward specification problem as classical RL. The paper should correct this to accurately state that while rewards remain externally specified, the policy/value functions become black-box, motivating the need for interpretability.

comments.title: VPN characterization is inaccurate
comments.quote: DRL like Value Prediction Network (VPN, Oh et al. (2017)) is self-supervised. Exploration-based RL algorithm is applied so that data are gathered real-time for training: the model thus optimizes its reward function.
comments.explanation: The characterization of VPN as self-supervised is misleading. VPN is a model-based RL method that learns a forward dynamics model to predict future states and rewards, then uses imagined rollouts for planning. The self-supervised aspect refers to learning the dynamics model, but VPN still requires external rewards from the environment for policy improvement. The paper conflates the dynamics model learning (self-supervised) with the overall RL objective (reward-based). This misrepresents how VPN actually works and creates an inaccurate comparison with SRD. Rewrite the first sentence to clarify that VPN uses self-supervised learning for dynamics modeling but still relies on environmental rewards for policy optimization.

comments.title: Convolution calculation error in fh neuron
comments.quote: When there is food near the fish, we get $y_{fh}=conv_{fh}(env)=[1,0,0]*[0.5,0,0]-0.5=0$ where $*$ denotes the convolution operator, so $a_{fh}=\sigma_{sa}(y_{fh})=1$.
comments.explanation: The convolution calculation is incorrect. For valid convolution with kernel [1,0,0] and input [0.5,0,0], the output should be 0.5, not 0. The bias subtraction should give 0.5 - 0.5 = 0, which is correct, but the convolution step itself is wrong. More importantly, the selective activation function $\sigma_{sa}(x) = \epsilon / (||x||^2 + \epsilon)$ would give $\sigma_{sa}(0) = 1$, but this creates a singularity where zero input produces maximum activation, which is counterintuitive for a food detection neuron. Rewrite the calculation as $y_{fh}=conv_{fh}(env)=[1,0,0]*[0.5,0,0]=0.5$, then $y_{fh}-0.5=0$, and reconsider whether zero input should produce maximum activation.

comments.title: Inconsistent failure mode labeling in Table 1
comments.quote: Table 1: Accuracy comparisons for Project A, project Compare A, project With-Lava-A and project Lava NOAV A.  $Acc = n_{reached\ target} / 4096$ . "No SRD" indicates no training: reasonable accuracy is attainable purely with design. SRD training generally increases the accuracies. Starred values reflect failure modes, matching their respective histograms; see fig. 6 and 7.
comments.explanation: The starred values in Table 1 are inconsistently labeled as failure modes. Compare A experiment 2 shows 0.589* and experiment 4 shows 0.422*, but With Lava A experiment 4 shows 0.762* despite being higher than the starred Compare A values. There's no clear threshold or criterion provided for what constitutes a failure mode. The text states these "matching their respective histograms" but doesn't explain the specific criteria. Rewrite the table caption to define the failure mode criteria: "Starred values indicate experiments where the cumulative histogram shows abnormal traversal patterns (e.g., excessive grass traversal or target avoidance), as detailed in Figures 6 and 7."

comments.title: Overstated interpretability consistency claim
comments.quote: While we have shown that our design provides a good consistency between the interpretable weights before and after training, it is not surprising that the combination of small differences in weights can yield different final weights that still perform well.
comments.explanation: The claim about 'good consistency' between interpretable weights before and after training is not supported by concrete evidence in the paper. The authors only provide anecdotal observations about weight patterns (Figure 5) without quantitative metrics to measure consistency. The statement that 'it is not surprising' that small differences yield different final weights contradicts the earlier claim of 'good consistency'. Either provide quantitative metrics (e.g., weight correlation coefficients, stability measures) or remove the unsupported consistency claim.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated efficiency claim
comments.quote: It is also efficient since very few weights are needed compared to traditional DNN.
comments.explanation: The efficiency claim is not substantiated with any computational analysis. The paper does not provide runtime comparisons, memory usage statistics, or FLOP counts against traditional DNNs. The claim that 'very few weights are needed' is only demonstrated on toy problems (1D fish and 2D lavaland) with extremely low-dimensional state spaces. Without empirical evidence on comparable problems, this efficiency claim remains speculative.

comments.title: Insufficient ethics statement
comments.quote: There is no specific ethical statement present. We demonstrate methods for designing neural networks for RL problem in a transparent way. We believe each part is transparently presented, and, if the paper is insufficient, the code in supp. materials should fully complements each description made in this paper.
comments.explanation: The ethics section is inadequate. It merely states there is 'no specific ethical statement' and deflects to transparency and supplementary code. For a machine learning paper, especially one involving reinforcement learning systems, this section should address potential ethical implications like algorithmic bias, safety considerations in autonomous systems, environmental impact of computational resources, and potential misuse of interpretable RL methods. The current statement fails to meet basic ethical disclosure standards expected in ML conferences.

comments.title: Insufficient reproducibility details
comments.quote: All codes are available in the supp. materials, to be released to public repository in case of acceptance. Results are tested on different random number seeds, and irregularities or mode collapses have been presented clearly in the paper as well (see for example the annotations made on the cumulative histograms).
comments.explanation: The reproducibility statement is too vague to enable proper replication. It mentions 'different random number seeds' but doesn't specify how many seeds were used, what the seed values were, or whether the results were averaged across runs. The reference to 'cumulative histograms' is unclear without specifying which figures contain these annotations. For proper reproducibility, the authors should state the exact number of random seeds used (e.g., '10 random seeds: 42, 123, 456, ...'), describe the statistical reporting method (mean ± std), and explicitly reference the figure numbers where irregularities are documented.

comments.title: Ambiguous parameter notation
comments.quote: Here, we use  $p_{dirt,grass} = 0.2$ , -0.8 chosen empirically.
comments.explanation: The parameter notation $p_{dirt,grass} = 0.2$, -0.8 is ambiguous. It appears to contain two values (0.2 and -0.8) but uses a single subscript. Based on the context of the paper, this likely represents separate parameters for dirt and grass tiles. Rewrite as "Here, we use $p_{dirt} = 0.2$ and $p_{grass} = -0.8$, chosen empirically" to clarify that these are two distinct parameters for different tile types.

comments.title: Inconsistent selective activation notation
comments.quote: so $a_{fh}=\sigma_{sa}(y_{fh})=1$
comments.explanation: The paper introduces the selective activation function as $\sigma_{sa}(x)$ but then uses $\tau_{sa}(y_{fh})$ in the calculation, while earlier defining it as $\sigma_{sa}(x)$. This notation inconsistency creates confusion. The function should be consistently denoted throughout. Use $\sigma_{sa}$ consistently: define as $\sigma_{sa}(x) = \epsilon / (||x||^2 + \epsilon)$ and use $a_{fh}=\sigma_{sa}(y_{fh})$ throughout.

comments.title: PFC threshold activation definition missing
comments.quote: define *threshold activation* as $\tau(x)=Tanh(LeakyReLU(x))$. Then PFC is constructed deliberately in the same way FishNN is constructed in an interpretable way as the following.
comments.explanation: The paper defines threshold activation as $\tau(x)=Tanh(LeakyReLU(x))$ but never explains the purpose of this specific composition or why it's called 'threshold activation'. Tanh(LeakyReLU(x)) doesn't create a clear threshold behavior - it's just a composition of two activation functions. If a threshold is needed, a step function or ReLU would be more appropriate. Either justify this specific choice or rename it to 'composed activation' to avoid confusion.

comments.title: Vague convergence limitation
comments.quote: So far, there is no guarantee for convergence either, and it may be difficult to provide a general proof, since the design is heavily dependent on the context.
comments.explanation: The statement about lack of convergence guarantees is too vague for a technical limitation. The paper should specify what type of convergence is lacking (e.g., weight convergence, policy convergence, reward convergence) and under what conditions. Since the method uses standard RL algorithms with modified reward functions, convergence properties should inherit from the underlying RL algorithms unless the self-reward mechanism introduces specific instability issues.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a 'Self Reward Design' (SRD) framework that relies on manual, fine-grained design of neural network weights and biases to achieve interpretability in reinforcement learning. While the approach provides transparency by construction, it lacks a rigorous evaluation of the claimed interpretability and relies on highly specialized, hand-crafted architectures that may not scale.

The paper makes an interesting attempt to bridge the gap between deep RL and explainability by moving away from post-hoc methods toward design-time transparency. However, the reliance on manually setting weights for specific tasks raises significant concerns about generalizability and the robustness of the 'interpretability' claims when faced with more complex state spaces.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Quantitative Interpretability Metrics: The authors define interpretability as the ability to manually manipulate weights and biases, but they provide no quantitative evidence that these weights remain semantically meaningful after the SRD training process. Section 5 claims that interpretability is 'preserved,' yet this is based on qualitative observation of weight heatmaps rather than a formal metric (e.g., neuron-level semantic fidelity or consistency under perturbation). Without a standard metric to measure the degradation of interpretability during gradient descent, the claim that the model remains 'highly interpretable' after optimization is anecdotal.

- Overfitting to Hand-Crafted Architectures: The methodology relies on the designer manually defining the structure of the neural network to match specific environmental features, such as the 'Food Location Detector' in Section 3 and 'tile-based modules' in Section 4. This approach is inherently brittle; it assumes the designer has a complete, perfect understanding of the environment's causal structure beforehand. The paper does not address how this design process would adapt to environments where the relevant features are not obvious or where the state space is high-dimensional and non-grid-like, limiting the framework's applicability to toy problems.

- Unclear Generalization of 'Self-Reward' Loss: The SRD loss function defined in Equation 2 and Equation 4 relies on a manually constructed PFC (Prefrontal Cortex) module that judges the agent's actions. The validity of the entire optimization process rests on the assumption that this hand-coded PFC is a perfect proxy for the 'true' task objective. If the PFC design is slightly flawed or misspecified, the agent will optimize toward a sub-optimal or unintended goal, effectively encoding the designer's biases into the agent's behavior. The paper fails to discuss the conditions under which this self-reward mechanism might diverge or lead to reward hacking, which is a primary concern for any self-supervised reinforcement learning loop.

- Incomplete Comparison to Existing XAI Baselines: The authors dismiss the need for comparisons with existing explainable RL methods by claiming their 'fine-grained' approach is fundamentally different. However, this avoids the necessary benchmarking against established techniques like reward decomposition or symbolic policy distillation. To be publishable at a top venue, the paper must demonstrate that the added manual design effort provides a tangible benefit in interpretability or performance compared to more automated, yet still interpretable, baselines.

- Lack of sensitivity analysis for the manual design parameters: The paper relies heavily on manually chosen weights and biases to ensure interpretability, yet it provides no analysis of how sensitive the agent's performance is to these specific design choices. A reader cannot determine if the 'fine-grained' interpretability is robust or if it collapses when the designer's initial parameters deviate slightly from the optimal configuration. I suggest adding a sensitivity analysis where the initial kernel weights in the tile-based modules are perturbed by varying degrees of noise. This would demonstrate whether the agent's ability to maintain its 'interpretable' behavior is a stable property of the architecture or merely a result of hyper-tuned initial conditions.

- Absence of a failure case study for the self-reward mechanism: The authors acknowledge that the PFC module is a hand-coded proxy for the task objective, but they do not explore the consequences of a misspecified PFC. If the PFC's internal logic is slightly misaligned with the environment's true reward structure, the SRD training could potentially lead to catastrophic performance degradation or reward hacking. The paper would benefit from a controlled experiment where the PFC is intentionally designed with a known bias or error—for example, misidentifying a specific tile type—to observe whether the agent exhibits predictable failure modes or if the self-reward loop compounds the error. This is essential to validate the claim that the system is 'highly interpretable' even when the designer's knowledge is imperfect.

- Need for a formal definition of interpretability degradation: While the paper claims interpretability is preserved after training, it relies on qualitative visual inspection of weight heatmaps. To make this claim rigorous, the authors should define a quantitative metric for interpretability degradation, such as the change in the activation patterns of specific neurons relative to their original, manually-defined purpose. Computing the cosine similarity between the pre-training and post-training weight vectors for the 'Food Location Detector' or 'tile-based modules' would provide a concrete measure of how much the 'fine-grained' meaning of these neurons is lost during the SRD optimization process.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents a novel concept but lacks the rigorous evaluation required to substantiate its claims about interpretability and scalability. To be acceptable, the authors must provide a more objective way to measure the 'fine-grained' interpretability and demonstrate that the approach can handle at least one task that is not a simple grid-world.

Revision targets:

- Develop and apply a quantitative metric to measure the semantic consistency of neurons before and after SRD training.

- Provide a formal analysis or a more complex simulation that demonstrates how the design process scales beyond manually defined grid-world features.

- Include a systematic comparison with at least one existing explainable RL method (e.g., reward decomposition or decision tree distillation) to justify the manual design overhead.

- Address the failure modes of the self-reward mechanism, specifically identifying how the system behaves when the hand-coded PFC module is misspecified.

comments.title: Ill-defined self-reward loss function
comments.quote:
s=CEL(z,argmax(z))$ (2)

where CEL is the Cross Entropy Loss and $z=\Sigma_{i=1}^{mem}[T,F]_{i}$ is the accumulated decision over $mem=8$ iterations
comments.explanation: The loss function as defined is mathematically trivial. In PyTorch, CEL(input, target) expects input to be logits and target to be the index of the correct class. By setting the target to argmax(z), the loss evaluates the cross-entropy between the distribution z and a one-hot distribution where the probability mass is already on the index with the maximum value. This results in a loss value near zero, providing no meaningful gradient for backpropagation. Please replace this with a loss function that compares the current decision z against an external or delayed reward signal.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of the selective activation function
comments.quote:
use 1D convolution layer and selective activation function as  $\sigma_{sa}(x) = \epsilon / (||x||^2 + \epsilon)$ , as the following.

<!-- PA
comments.explanation: The definition of \sigma_{sa}(x) as \epsilon / (||x||^2 + \epsilon) is problematic because it is a function of the norm of the input x, yet it is applied to the scalar output of a convolution y_{fh}. If x is a scalar, the function is symmetric around 0 and peaks at x=0. However, the authors define conv_{fh} such that y_{fh} = 0 when food is present. If the convolution output deviates from 0 due to noise, the activation decreases. This function does not distinguish between positive and negative deviations from the target bias. Please rewrite the definition to explicitly account for the bias-centered target, such as \sigma_{sa}(x) = \epsilon / ((x - b)^2 + \epsilon), to clarify how the neuron selectively activates at the desired bias.

comments.title: Logical inconsistency in training claims
comments.quote: Our robot (representing any artificial agent) rewards itself through purposeful design of DNN architecture, enabling it to solve problem without training.
